subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $M$ and charge Q moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ describe a circular path of radius $\mathrm{R}$ when subjected to a uniform transverse magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$. The work done by the field when the particle completes one full circle is
question: A particle of mass $M$ and charge Q moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ describe a circular path of radius $\mathrm{R}$ when subjected to a uniform transverse magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$. The work done by the field when the particle completes one full circle is
solution: Force is $\perp^{\mathrm{r}}$ to displacement $\Rightarrow$ the work done is zero

q: A current loop, having two circular arcs joined by two radial lines is shown in the figure. It carries a current of 10 A. The magnetic field at point O will be close to:
question: A current loop, having two circular arcs joined by two radial lines is shown in the figure. It carries a current of 10 A. The magnetic field at point O will be close to:
solution:
Magnetic field due to PQ and RS are zero at ' O '.

Magnetic field due to QR

BQR=μ04π103×10-2π4 (outwards)

Magnetic field due to SP

BSP=μ04π105×10-2π4 (inwards)

Net field B=μo4π10π4×10-213-15

=1.0×10-5T

q: A coil having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> turns is wound tightly in the form of a spiral with inner and outer radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> respectively. Find the magnetic field at centre, when a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> passes through coil :
question: A coil having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> turns is wound tightly in the form of a spiral with inner and outer radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi></math> respectively. Find the magnetic field at centre, when a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> passes through coil :
solution: Recall the formula of the magnitude of the magnetic field in terms of current and permeability in free space, B=∫abμ0dNi2x=∫abμ0Nb-adxi2x=μ0Ni2(b-a)lnba.

subject: Physics
q: A charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is spread uniformly over an insulated loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>. If it is rotated with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi></math> with respect to normal axis then magnetic moment of the loop is:
question: A charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is spread uniformly over an insulated loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>. If it is rotated with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi></math> with respect to normal axis then magnetic moment of the loop is:
solution: ML=q2m⇒M=q2m×mr2ω⇒M=qωr22.

subject: Physics
q:
A proton moving with a constant velocity passes through a region of space without any change in its velocity. If E→ and B→ represent the electric and magnetic fields respectively, then the region of space may have :
(A) E=0, B=0;  (B) E=0, B≠0;  (C) E≠0, B=0;  (D) E≠0, B≠0

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
A proton moving with a constant velocity passes through a region of space without any change in its velocity. If E→ and B→ represent the electric and magnetic fields respectively, then the region of space may have :
(A) E=0, B=0;  (B) E=0, B≠0;  (C) E≠0, B=0;  (D) E≠0, B≠0

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution:
Net force on proton inside an electric and a magnetic field is given by

F→e+F→m=qE→+qv→×B→    ...1

If both E→ and B→ are zero, then F→e and F→m both are zero. Hence, velocity may remain constant. Therefore, option (A) is correct.

If E=0, B≠0, but velocity is parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field, then also F→e and F→m both are zero. Hence, option (B) is also correct.

If E≠0, B≠0, but F→e + F→m=0, then also velocity may remain constant. Thus, option (D) is also correct.

subject: Physics
q: A conductor lies along the z-axis at - 1.5 ≤ z <1.5 m  and carries a fixed current of 10.0 A in - a ^ z  direction (see figure). For a field B → = 3.0 × 1 0 - 4  e - 0.2x  a ^ y  T, find the power required to move the conductor at constant speed to x = 2.0 m, y = 0 m in 5 × 1 0 - 3  s . Assume parallel motion along the x-axis.
question: A conductor lies along the z-axis at - 1.5 ≤ z <1.5 m  and carries a fixed current of 10.0 A in - a ^ z  direction (see figure). For a field B → = 3.0 × 1 0 - 4  e - 0.2x  a ^ y  T, find the power required to move the conductor at constant speed to x = 2.0 m, y = 0 m in 5 × 1 0 - 3  s . Assume parallel motion along the x-axis.
solution:
Power P = W Δ t

Force on conductor
F→=I(l→×b→)

F → =-10( 3 a ^ z )×( 3× 10 −4 e −0.2x a ^ y )

⇒F→=90×10-4(e-0.2x) along with x-axis

work done on the conductor in moving along x-axis.

W=∫x=02F→.dx→

W = ∫ 0 2 Bi ℓ dx = ∫ 0 2 3 × 10 - 4 e - 0.2x × 3 × 10dx =90×10-4e-0.2x-0.202

= 450 × 10 - 4 [ 1 - e - 0.4 ] = 180 × 10 - 4 ( Using e - x = 1 - x )

P = W Δ t = 180 × 10 - 4 5 × 10 - 3 = 3.6 W.

Hence, closest option is 2.97 W.
(if we used exact value of exponential term will get exact answer).

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet with a magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is placed in parallel position relative to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amount of required work done in turning the magnet form parallel to antiparallel position relative to the field direction is ________.
question: A bar magnet with a magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is placed in parallel position relative to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The amount of required work done in turning the magnet form parallel to antiparallel position relative to the field direction is ________.
solution:
For a bar magnet, the work done in turning from parallel to antiparallel position relative to the field direction is 

W=MBcosθ1-cosθ2, here, θ1=0° and θ1=180°.

W=-MBcos180°-mBcos0°

W=2 MB=2×5×0.4=4 J

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop of radius $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ at a point on the axis at a distance of $4 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre is $54 \mu \mathrm{T}$. What will be its value at the centre of the Loop?
question: The magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop of radius $3 \mathrm{~cm}$ at a point on the axis at a distance of $4 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre is $54 \mu \mathrm{T}$. What will be its value at the centre of the Loop?
solution:
Using formula $B=\frac{\mu_0^{\text {II }}}{2\left(R^2+X^2\right)^{3 / 2}}$, we get
$$
54=\frac{\mu_0 i(3)^2}{2\left[(3)^2+(4)^2\right]^{3 / 2}}
$$
At the centre of the coil, $X=0$ and $B=\frac{\mu_0 i}{2(3)}$
Using equation (i)
$$
B=\frac{54 \times 5^3}{(3)^2 \times 3} \Rightarrow B=250 \mu \mathrm{T} \text {. }
$$

subject: Physics
q: $1\,\mu$C charge moving with velocity $\vec{v} = \left(\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}\right)$ m/s in the region of magnetic field $\vec{B} = \left(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 5\hat{k}\right)$ T. The magnitude of force acting on it is $\sqrt{\alpha} \times 10^{-6}$ N. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: $1\,\mu$C charge moving with velocity $\vec{v} = \left(\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}\right)$ m/s in the region of magnetic field $\vec{B} = \left(2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 5\hat{k}\right)$ T. The magnitude of force acting on it is $\sqrt{\alpha} \times 10^{-6}$ N. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
The magnetic force acting on a moving charge is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Given:
$q = 1 \, \mu\text{C} = 10^{-6} \text{ C}$
$\vec{v} = \hat{i} - 2\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$
$\vec{B} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 5\hat{k}$

Calculating the cross product $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$:
$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & -2 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & -5 \end{vmatrix}$

$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = \hat{i}(10 - 9) - \hat{j}(-5 - 6) + \hat{k}(3 + 4)$
$\vec{v} \times \vec{B} = \hat{i} + 11\hat{j} + 7\hat{k}$

The magnitude of $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ is:
$|\vec{v} \times \vec{B}| = \sqrt{1^2 + 11^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{1 + 121 + 49} = \sqrt{171}$

The magnitude of the force is:
$|\vec{F}| = q |\vec{v} \times \vec{B}| = 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{171} = \sqrt{171} \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$

Comparing this with the given expression $\sqrt{\alpha} \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$, we get:
$\alpha = 171$

Answer: $171$

subject: Physics
q: Let $B_1$ be the magnitude of magnetic field at center of a circular coil of radius $R$ carrying current $I$. Let $B_2$ be the magnitude of magnetic field at an axial distance ' $x$ ' from the center. For $x: R=3: 4, \frac{B_2}{B_1}$ is :
question: Let $B_1$ be the magnitude of magnetic field at center of a circular coil of radius $R$ carrying current $I$. Let $B_2$ be the magnitude of magnetic field at an axial distance ' $x$ ' from the center. For $x: R=3: 4, \frac{B_2}{B_1}$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \mathrm{R}} \qquad \mathrm{B}_2=\mathrm{B}_1 \sin ^3 \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{~B}_2}{\mathrm{~B}_1}=\sin ^3 \theta=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^3=\frac{64}{125}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : A electron in a certain region of uniform magnetic field is moving with constant velocity in a straight line path.
Reason (R): The magnetic field in that region is along the direction of velocity of the electron. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : A electron in a certain region of uniform magnetic field is moving with constant velocity in a straight line path.
Reason (R): The magnetic field in that region is along the direction of velocity of the electron. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
solution: If the electron's velocity is along the direction of magnetic field then magnetic force on electron is zero and it will not accelerate.

q: A single turn current loop in the shape of a right angle triangle with sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The loop is in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> whose direction is parallel to the current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side of the loop. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>130</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
question: A single turn current loop in the shape of a right angle triangle with sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The loop is in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> whose direction is parallel to the current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side of the loop. The magnitude of the magnetic force on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> side will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>130</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Here, B=0.75 T

The magnitude of magnetic force on the 5 cm side is FAB=i×lABBsinθ           angle between AC & AB

=2×5100×0.75×1213

=9130 N

Hence, the value of x=9.

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius $R$ is $16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$. The magnetic field at a distance $x=\sqrt{3} R$ on its axis from the centre is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{T}$.
question: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius $R$ is $16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$. The magnetic field at a distance $x=\sqrt{3} R$ on its axis from the centre is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{T}$.
solution:
The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $B_{center} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} = 16\mu$ T.
On the axis at distance $x$ from center: $B(x) = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$.
At $x = \sqrt{3}R$: $B(\sqrt{3}R) = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + 3R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(4R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{16R}$.
The ratio: $\frac{B(\sqrt{3}R)}{B_{center}} = \frac{1/16R}{1/2R} = \frac{1}{8}$.
Therefore: $B(\sqrt{3}R) = \frac{1}{8} \times 16\mu = 2\mu$ T.

subject: Physics
q: A circular coil having 200 turns, $2.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ area and carrying $100 \mu \mathrm{A}$ current is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 1T. Initially the magnetic dipole moment $(\vec{M})$ was directed along $\vec{B}$. Amount of work, required to rotate the coil through $90^{\circ}$ from its initial orientation such that $\vec{M}$ becomes perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, is ______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
question: A circular coil having 200 turns, $2.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ area and carrying $100 \mu \mathrm{A}$ current is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 1T. Initially the magnetic dipole moment $(\vec{M})$ was directed along $\vec{B}$. Amount of work, required to rotate the coil through $90^{\circ}$ from its initial orientation such that $\vec{M}$ becomes perpendicular to $\vec{B}$, is ______ $\mu \mathrm{J}$.
solution: We know $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{W}_{\text {ext }} & =\Delta \mathrm{U}+\Delta \mathrm{KE} \quad(\text { P.E. }=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}) \\ & =-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{f}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{i}}+0 \\ & =-\mathrm{MB} \cos 90+\mathrm{MB} \cos 0 \\ & =\mathrm{MB} \\ & =\mathrm{NIAB} \\ & =200 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{5}{2} \times 10^{-4} \times 1=5 \mu \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a co-axial straight cable, the central conductor and the outer conductor carry equal currents in opposite directions. The magnetic field is zero :
question: In a co-axial straight cable, the central conductor and the outer conductor carry equal currents in opposite directions. The magnetic field is zero :
solution: $\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}} \cdot \mathrm{d} \vec{\ell}=\mu_0 \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{enc}}=0$ $\therefore \mathrm{B}=0$ outside the cable

subject: Physics
q:
A velocity selector consists of electric field E→=E k^ and magnetic field B→=B j^ with B=12 mT. The value E required for an electron of energy 728eV moving along the positive x-axis to pass undeflected is 

(Given, mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg)
question:
A velocity selector consists of electric field E→=E k^ and magnetic field B→=B j^ with B=12 mT. The value E required for an electron of energy 728eV moving along the positive x-axis to pass undeflected is 

(Given, mass of electron =9.1×10-31 kg)
solution:
Fiven that E→=Ek^ and B→=12 j^ mT

Kinetic energy=728 eV

Kinetic energy =12mv2

⇒728 eV=12×9.1×10-31×v2

⇒728×1.6×10-19=12×9.1×10-31×v2

⇒v=16×106 m s-1

For electron to move undeflected net force on it should be zero.

⇒eE=evB

⇒E=vB=16×106×12×10-3

⇒E=192×103 V m-1=192 kV m-1

subject: Physics
q:
The temperature of 3.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas is increased by 40.0°C without changing the pressure of the gas. The molecules in the gas rotate but do not oscillate. If the ratio of change in internal energy of the gas to the amount of workdone by the gas is x10. Then the value of x (round off to the nearest integer) is _________.

( GivenR=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
question:
The temperature of 3.00 mol of an ideal diatomic gas is increased by 40.0°C without changing the pressure of the gas. The molecules in the gas rotate but do not oscillate. If the ratio of change in internal energy of the gas to the amount of workdone by the gas is x10. Then the value of x (round off to the nearest integer) is _________.

( GivenR=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
solution:
First, recall the formula of change in internal energy in terms of molar specific heat at constant volume and change in temperature, ∆U=nCVΔT, for diatomic gas, CV=f2R=52R, ⇒∆U=n×52×RΔT

⇒ΔUW=52nRΔTnRΔT=52=2510

So, x=25

subject: Physics
q: A long solenoid carrying a current produces a magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> along its axis. If the current is doubled and the number of turns per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> is halved, the new value of magnetic field will be equal to
question: A long solenoid carrying a current produces a magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> along its axis. If the current is doubled and the number of turns per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> is halved, the new value of magnetic field will be equal to
solution:
Magnetic field due to long solenoid along its axis is given as,

B=μ0nI, where n is the number of turns in the solenoid per unit length, I is current in the solenoid and μ0 is a constant called the permeability of free space.

⇒B1 B2=n1I1n2I2

According to the question, n2=n12 and I2=2I1

⇒BB2=n1n12×I12I1⇒B2=B; means, the magnetic field remains the same.

And IR=VRResistance of loop=0

subject: Physics
q: A charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> accelerated by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> enters a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mT</mi></math> at right angle to the direction of field. The charge particle completes semicircle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> inside magnetic field. The mass of the charge particle is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>.
question: A charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> accelerated by a potential difference of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> enters a region of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mT</mi></math> at right angle to the direction of field. The charge particle completes semicircle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> inside magnetic field. The mass of the charge particle is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>.
solution:
As we know, radius of the circular path is given by,

r=mvqB=2mKqB=2×m×qVqB

⇒r2=2mVqB2

⇒m=r2qB22V=3×10-22×2×10-6×4×10-322×100=144×10-18 kg

subject: Physics
q: A proton and a deuteron are both accelerated through the same potential difference and enter in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of the field. If the deuteron follows a path of radius $R$, assuming the neutron and proton masses are nearly equal, the radius of the proton's path will be
question: A proton and a deuteron are both accelerated through the same potential difference and enter in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of the field. If the deuteron follows a path of radius $R$, assuming the neutron and proton masses are nearly equal, the radius of the proton's path will be
solution:
As charge on both proton and deuteron is same i.e. ' $e$ '
Energy acquired by both, $E=\mathrm{eV}$ For Deuteron.
Kinetic energy, $\frac{1}{2} m V^2=e V$
[ $V$ is the potential difference]
$$
v=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m_d}}
$$
But $m_d=2 m$
Therefore, $v=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{2 m}}=\sqrt{\frac{e V}{m}}$
Radius of path, $R=\frac{m v}{e B}$
Substituting value of ' $v$ ' we get
$$
\begin{array}{r}
R=\frac{2 m \sqrt{\frac{e v}{m}}}{e B} \\
\frac{R}{2}=\frac{m \sqrt{\frac{e v}{m}}}{e B}
\end{array}
$$
For proton :
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} m V^2=e V \\
& V=\sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}} \\
& \text { Radius of path, } R^{\prime}=\frac{m V}{e B}=\frac{m \sqrt{\frac{2 e V}{m}}}{e B} \\
& R^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} \times \frac{R}{2} \quad \text { [From eq. (i)] } \\
& R^{\prime}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
Two long parallel conductors S1 and S2 are separated by a distance 10 cm and carrying currents of 4 A and 2 A respectively. The conductors are placed along x-axis in X-Y plane. There is a point P located between the conductors (as shown in figure).
A charge particle of 3π coulomb is passing through the point P with velocity v→=2i^+3j^ m s-1; where i^ & j^ represents unit vector along x & yaxis respectively.
The force acting on the charge particle is 4π×10-5-xi^+2j^ N. The value of x is
question:
Two long parallel conductors S1 and S2 are separated by a distance 10 cm and carrying currents of 4 A and 2 A respectively. The conductors are placed along x-axis in X-Y plane. There is a point P located between the conductors (as shown in figure).
A charge particle of 3π coulomb is passing through the point P with velocity v→=2i^+3j^ m s-1; where i^ & j^ represents unit vector along x & yaxis respectively.
The force acting on the charge particle is 4π×10-5-xi^+2j^ N. The value of x is
solution:
Net magnetic field at P is BNet=μ0×i12πr1−μ0i22πr2-k^

BNet =μ042π×4100-μ022π×6100

=μ0π×10012-16

=μ0π×10042×6

=μ0×1003π-k^

So, force acting on charge particle isF→=qv→×B→=3π2i^+3j^μ0×1003π-k^

=4π×10-72i^+3j^×100-k^

=4π×10-52j^-3i^

∴  x=3

subject: Physics
q:
If the maximum value of accelerating potential provided by a radio frequency oscillator is 12kV. The number of revolution made by a proton in a cyclotron to achieve one sixth of the speed of light is: 

mp=1.67×10-27 kg, e=1.6×10-19 C, Speed of light=3×108 m s-1
question:
If the maximum value of accelerating potential provided by a radio frequency oscillator is 12kV. The number of revolution made by a proton in a cyclotron to achieve one sixth of the speed of light is: 

mp=1.67×10-27 kg, e=1.6×10-19 C, Speed of light=3×108 m s-1
solution:
From the conservation of energy,

2neV=12mv2

⇒2n×12×103×1.6×10-19=12×1.67×10-27×5×1072

⇒n=543

q: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is kept in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mi>Z</mi></math> plane with its centre at origin. A long wire carrying the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is placed parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis and passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. The magnitude of the torque on the loop about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis is given by.
question: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is kept in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mi>Z</mi></math> plane with its centre at origin. A long wire carrying the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is placed parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis and passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>)</mo></math>. The magnitude of the torque on the loop about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math>-axis is given by.
solution:
Net force acting on wire AB & CD are zero

F1=ILB=I×2a×μ0I2πa2+b2=μ0I2aπa2+b2

τ=F1cosθ×2a=μ0I2aπa2+b2×ba2+b2×2a

τ=2μ0I2a2bπa2+b2≈2μ0I2a2πb,  a≪b

question: A proton, a deuteron and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle with same kinetic energy enter into a uniform magnetic field at right angle to magnetic field. The ratio of the radii of their respective circular paths is :
solution:
Radius of circular path in magnetic field is given by 

R=mvqB=m2KEqBm∝mq

RP:RD:Rα=11:21:42=1:2:1

The magnitude of the other field (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mT</mi></math>) is close to
question: A magnetic dipole is acted upon by two magnetic fields which are inclined to each other at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>75</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math>. One of the fields has a magnitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mT</mi></math>. The dipole attains stable equilibrium at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> with this field. The magnitude of the other field (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mT</mi></math>) is close to
solution:
θ 1 +θ= 75 o

M B 1 sin θ 1 =M B 2 sinθ

⇒ B 1 2 = 15 2

⇒ B 1 = 15 2 ≈11 mT

q: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ from axis of the wire is :
question: A long straight wire of radius a carries a steady current I. The current is uniformly distributed across its cross section. The ratio of the magnetic field at $\frac{a}{2}$ and $2 a$ from axis of the wire is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_1 2 \pi \frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}=\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{~B}_1=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \\ & \mathrm{B}_2 2 \pi 2 \mathrm{a}=\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I} \\ & \mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}}\end{aligned}$

q: A singly ionized magnesium atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> ion is accelerated to kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>keV</mi></math>, and is projected perpendicularly into a magnetic field $B$ of the magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The radius of path formed will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A singly ionized magnesium atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> ion is accelerated to kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>keV</mi></math>, and is projected perpendicularly into a magnetic field $B$ of the magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The radius of path formed will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
The radius of circle formed by a charged particle in a magnetic field perpendicular to the velocity is given by,

R=mVqB⇒R=2mKqB=2×24×1.6×10-27×5000×1.6×10-191.6×10-19×0.5=979×10-4 m≃10 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two parallel long current carrying wire separated by a distance $2 r$ are shown in the figure. The ratio of magnetic field at $A$ to the magnetic field produced at $C$ is $\frac{x}{7}$. The value of $x$ is _____
question: Two parallel long current carrying wire separated by a distance $2 r$ are shown in the figure. The ratio of magnetic field at $A$ to the magnetic field produced at $C$ is $\frac{x}{7}$. The value of $x$ is _____
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}+\frac{\mu_0(2 \mathrm{i})}{2 \pi(3 \mathrm{r})}=\frac{5 \mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{6 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mu_0(2 \mathrm{i})}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}+\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{2 \pi(3 \mathrm{r})}=\frac{7 \mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{6 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{5}{7} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{x}=5\end{aligned}$

It gets an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by an applied magnetic field (west to east). The value of magnetic field: (Rest mass of proton is <math><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> )
question: Proton with kinetic energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> moves from south to north. It gets an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> by an applied magnetic field (west to east). The value of magnetic field: (Rest mass of proton is <math><mn>1.6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> )
solution:
∴K.E.=1.6×10-13=12×1.6×10-27v2
v=2×107m/s
∴Bqv=ma
B=1.6×10-27×10121.6×10-19×2×107
=0.71×10-3T
So,

B=0.71 mT

subject: Physics
q: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
question: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is:
solution:
Two identical circular loops P and Q, each of radius $r$, are separated by distance $r$ along their common axis. Current in P is $I$ and in Q is $4I$ (both clockwise as seen from O).
Point O is at the midpoint. The magnetic fields from each loop at O:
$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from P, pointing toward Q)
$B_2 = \frac{4\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}}$ (from Q, pointing toward P)
$B_{net} = B_2 - B_1 = \frac{3\mu_0 I R^2}{2(2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}R}$
Direction: towards Q.

subject: Physics
q:
Consider a moving coil galvanomenter (MCG):
A. The torsional constant in moving coil galvanometer has dimentions $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
B. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.
C. If we increase number of turns $(\mathrm{N})$ to its double $(2 \mathrm{~N})$, then the voltage sensitivity doubles.
D. MCG can be converted into an ammeter by introducing a shunt resistance of large value in parallel with galvanometer.
E. Current sensitivity of MCG depends inversely on number of turns of coil.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider a moving coil galvanomenter (MCG):
A. The torsional constant in moving coil galvanometer has dimentions $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
B. Increasing the current sensitivity may not necessarily increase the voltage sensitivity.
C. If we increase number of turns $(\mathrm{N})$ to its double $(2 \mathrm{~N})$, then the voltage sensitivity doubles.
D. MCG can be converted into an ammeter by introducing a shunt resistance of large value in parallel with galvanometer.
E. Current sensitivity of MCG depends inversely on number of turns of coil.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) $\tau=\mathrm{C} \theta \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=[\mathrm{C}][1]$
(B) $\mathbf{C} . \mathrm{S}=\frac{\theta}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{\mathrm{BNA}}{\mathrm{C}}$;
V.S. $=\frac{\mathrm{BNA}}{\mathrm{RC}}[\mathrm{R}$ also depends on ' N ']
(C) V.S. $\propto \frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{CR}} \quad \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{NR}$
(D) False [Theory]
(E) E [False]
$\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{S} \propto \mathrm{~N}$
$\Rightarrow \because \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{S} .=\frac{\mathrm{NAB}}{\mathrm{C}}$

subject: Physics
q: In a certain region static electric and magnetic fields exist. The magnetic field is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>. If a test charge moving with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> experiences no force in that region, then the electric field in the region, in SI units, is:
question: In a certain region static electric and magnetic fields exist. The magnetic field is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math>. If a test charge moving with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> experiences no force in that region, then the electric field in the region, in SI units, is:
solution:
The total force experienced by test charge is Fe→+ F→m=0.

Fe=-Fm

Fe=−q0v0B0V→×B→

Fe=-q0 v0B03i^- j^+2k^×i^+2j^-4k^

F→e= -qv0B0 (14j^ +7k^)

The electric field produced by the test charge is ∵   E→= f→eq= -v0B0 ( 14j^+7k^)

subject: Physics
q: A square loop is carrying a steady current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> and the magnitude of its magnetic dipole moment is <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . If this square loop is changed to a circular loop and it carries the same current, the magnitude of the magnetic dipole moment of circular loop will be:
question: A square loop is carrying a steady current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> and the magnitude of its magnetic dipole moment is <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> . If this square loop is changed to a circular loop and it carries the same current, the magnitude of the magnetic dipole moment of circular loop will be:
solution:
Let the side of square loop be a.
The magnetic dipole is∴m=Ia2
a=mI
Radius of circular loop r related to a as circumference of circular loop is equal to perimeter of square loop.
2πr=4a=4mI
r=2πmI
∴ New dipole moment m'=π2πmI2I
=4mπ
